skill.name: azure-appconfiguration-ts
skill.description: Build TypeScript applications with Azure App Configuration, feature flags, dynamic refresh, labels, snapshots, and Key Vault references.
skill.summary: Use Azure App Configuration SDK patterns in TypeScript applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but the external command, weak crypto, certificate, and network scanning hits are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, Azure SDK names, and ordinary TypeScript examples. The real publishable risk is that the skill documents connection-string secrets, environment variables, Key Vault references, and console logging of configuration values, so users need clear secret-handling caution.

medium_findings.title: Connection String Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for sensitive credential guidance. The skill shows an Azure App Configuration connection string containing a Secret field and then demonstrates constructing a client from that environment variable. This is legitimate Azure SDK usage, but connection strings are high-value credentials and should be replaced with managed identity where possible.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference a connection string Secret field and use it for authentication. The surrounding text also recommends DefaultAzureCredential, so this is a moderate guidance risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Configuration Values Logged To Console
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for unsafe example handling of configuration values. The examples print configuration values and snapshot values, and one nearby example retrieves a database password from Key Vault-backed configuration. There is no evidence of exfiltration, but users could copy these examples and expose sensitive values in logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly print values returned from configuration APIs and show a resolved secret value. This is a real defensive concern, although the file is documentation and does not itself execute the logging.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The static analyzer treated Markdown triple backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are fenced documentation examples, not code executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reported external command location is inside a Markdown fenced block. The file contains no executable script wrapper, shell expansion, or runtime command execution path.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Azure URLs Are Expected Network Configuration
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE as a malicious network finding. The hardcoded URL is a placeholder Azure App Configuration endpoint, and the connection string is an example format for the documented Azure service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL uses an Azure App Configuration resource pattern and is presented as an environment variable example. No external collection endpoint or data transfer code is present.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Only High Severity Hits Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The weak cryptography, certificate or key file, and network scanning findings are keyword matches against product terms such as configuration, feature flags, setting keys, and ConfigurationMapFeatureFlagProvider. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, certificate file access, or network scanning behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual line review shows ordinary Azure SDK documentation and property names. The reported high severity categories do not match the semantic behavior of the file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Bearing Connection String Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a connection string example with a Secret component and shows using that value from an environment variable. This pattern is legitimate but should be treated as sensitive credential handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential format and environment variable use are explicit. The risk is user misuse, not malicious behavior by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Configuration Logging Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates printing configuration values to standard output. Configuration stores often contain secrets or operational values, so copied examples can leak data to logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The console logging examples are direct and easy to verify. The impact depends on what users store in App Configuration.

content.value_statement: TypeScript teams need clear patterns for centralized configuration, feature flags, and runtime refresh. This skill provides Azure SDK examples for App Configuration, Key Vault references, labels, snapshots, and feature management.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation commands for Azure App Configuration, identity, provider, and feature management packages.
Demonstrates AppConfigurationClient authentication with DefaultAzureCredential and connection strings.
Provides examples for creating, reading, updating, deleting, locking, and unlocking settings.
Explains provider-based loading, dynamic refresh, labels, snapshots, and key prefix trimming.
Covers feature flag creation and evaluation with Microsoft feature management.
Includes Key Vault reference loading with secret refresh settings.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run validation against a live Azure resource.
It does not include a complete production application template.
It assumes the user already has Azure credentials and App Configuration access.
It does not cover every Azure App Configuration SDK option or error case.

use_cases.title: Centralize Runtime Configuration
use_cases.description: Move TypeScript application settings into Azure App Configuration with labels, prefixes, and provider-based loading.

use_cases.title: Roll Out Features Gradually
use_cases.description: Create and evaluate feature flags with targeting rules for users, groups, and rollout percentages.

use_cases.title: Manage Release Configuration
use_cases.description: Use snapshots and labels to separate release, development, and production configuration states.

prompt_templates.title: Install The Packages
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the packages I need for Azure App Configuration in a TypeScript application, and explain when to use each package.

prompt_templates.title: Load Application Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me load Azure App Configuration settings in TypeScript with DefaultAzureCredential, labels, and trimmed key prefixes.

prompt_templates.title: Add Feature Flags
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript feature flag setup using Azure App Configuration and Microsoft feature management with targeting context.

prompt_templates.title: Design Refresh And Secrets
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production TypeScript pattern for dynamic refresh, Key Vault references, snapshots, and safe secret handling.

output_examples.input: I need to read production settings from Azure App Configuration.
output_examples.output: The skill would guide you to use the provider package, select production-labeled keys, trim application prefixes, and access values through a configuration map.

output_examples.input: I want a beta feature for selected users.
output_examples.output: The skill would outline a feature flag with targeting rules for users and groups, then evaluate it through the feature manager.

output_examples.input: I need immutable configuration for a release.
output_examples.output: The skill would describe creating a snapshot, listing settings from that snapshot, and loading the snapshot through the provider.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential and Azure RBAC over long-lived connection strings.
Use labels, prefixes, and snapshots to separate environments and releases.
Avoid logging raw configuration values, especially values resolved from Key Vault.
anti_patterns: Do not commit Azure App Configuration connection strings or secrets to source control.
Do not treat feature flags as authorization controls for protected operations.
Do not refresh configuration on every request without considering latency and service limits.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It provides TypeScript SDK examples for applications that use existing Azure App Configuration resources.

faq.question: Which authentication method does it recommend?
faq.answer: It shows DefaultAzureCredential as the recommended approach and also documents connection string usage.

faq.question: Can it help with feature flags?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers low-level feature flag creation and evaluation with Microsoft feature management.

faq.question: Does it support dynamic refresh?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes provider refresh options, refresh intervals, callbacks, and an Express middleware pattern.

faq.question: Does it cover Key Vault references?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to load Key Vault references and configure secret refresh intervals.

faq.question: Is it safe for production guidance?
faq.answer: It is useful for production planning, but users should harden secret handling, logging, RBAC, and error handling.
